description: Cross-registry DNS resolution that returns multiple IP addresses, where one is public and at least one is in a forbidden range, MUST reject the ENTIRE resolution. An implementation MUST NOT silently filter out the forbidden address(es) and connect to the remaining safe one(s). Filter-and-proceed is explicitly non-conformant: an attacker who controls both a public IP and a private target can return both addresses, and a filter-based resolver would proceed to the private target on a retry, reconnect, or DNS-rebinding second resolution. The IP-range filter is all-or-nothing per hostname (RFC-ACDP-0006 §7.1, mixed-answer rejection).

input.scenario: Resolver dereferences an acdp:// reference whose authority resolves to a DNS answer set containing one public and one private address

expected.behavior: The resolver MUST reject the whole resolution for the hostname the moment it observes a single forbidden address in the answer set — before any TCP connect. It MUST NOT keep the public address(es) and proceed. Non-conformant: resolve("attacker.example") -> ["203.0.113.10","10.0.0.1"] -> filter to ["203.0.113.10"] -> connect to 203.0.113.10. Required: validate all -> 10.0.0.1 is forbidden -> reject the resolution -> no connection made.
expected.non_conformant_implementation: A SafeDnsResolver (or equivalent DNS hook) that returns the surviving safe addresses after dropping the forbidden ones, instead of failing the resolution outright.
expected.rationale: Partial filtering defeats the SSRF defense. The forbidden address remains in DNS; a retry, a connection-pool reconnect, or a TOCTOU second resolution can still route to the private target. Rejecting the entire resolution is the only behavior that holds under reconnect and rebinding.
